title: Otitis Media
oneLiner: Acute middle ear infection, most common in young children but all ages at risk; if missed can cause hearing loss, mastoiditis, or intracranial spread.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 2‑year‑old boy presents to the ED with a 2‑day history of fever (38.9 °C), irritability, and pulling at his right ear. His mother says he has had a runny nose for the past week. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is bulging, opaque, and erythematous, with decreased mobility on pneumatic insufflation. He has mild purulent discharge in the ear canal (from a spontaneous perforation). No post‑auricular swelling. He is otherwise well‑appearing.

**Dissect the vignette (pattern clues):**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age (2 years) | Peak age for acute otitis media (AOM) — 6 months–3 years; Eustachian tube is short, horizontal, and prone to dysfunction |
| Recent upper respiratory infection (coryza) | Viral upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) causes Eustachian tube oedema → impaired middle ear drainage → negative pressure → effusion |
| Fever + ear pulling | Classic combination: fever indicates systemic inflammation; ear pulling is a non‑specific localising sign (babies can’t localise pain) |
| Bulging + opaque TM | Bulging = pus under pressure; opacity = purulent fluid (rather than serous effusion) |
| Erythematous TM | Hyperaemia from inflammation |
| Decreased TM mobility | Confirms presence of fluid/pus in middle ear space |
| Spontaneous otorrhea | Perforation of TM → pus draining into ear canal; often decompresses pain (child may suddenly improve) |
| No post‑auricular swelling | Unlikely mastoiditis (makes sense as this is uncomplicated AOM) |
| Well‑appearing | No signs of systemic toxicity → can be managed without immediate IV antibiotics |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The afebrile adult:** A 30‑year‑old with deep earache, normal temperature, and mild TM injection. Could be early AOM, but also think of TMJ dysfunction, dental pain, or otitis externa. The key clue: TM mobility is reduced (pus) vs normal in non‑infectious causes.
- **The immunocompromised patient:** Neutropenic or HIV‑positive patient with no fever, minimal ear pain, but a bulging TM with necrotic appearance. This is a medical emergency — pseudomonal or fungal infection can rapidly cause malignant otitis externa or extend intracranially.
- **The “grumpy baby”:** A 6‑month‑old with only poor feeding and crying, no fever, no ear pulling. Parents may not notice ear symptoms. Always complete an otoscopic exam on any febrile infant with unexplained irritability.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Otitis media is inflammation of the middle ear mucosa. When it is acute and infected (acute otitis media, AOM), a pocket of pus builds up behind the eardrum, causing pain, fever, and hearing loss. It is distinct from otitis media with effusion (OME), where fluid sits behind the TM without acute infection.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** AOM is the most common bacterial infection in children — about 80% of children will have at least one episode by age 3. It accounts for massive antibiotic use and clinic visits.
- **Stakes:** Hearing loss from AOM or persistent OME can delay language development. Complications — mastoiditis, meningitis, brain abscess — are rare but devastating.
- **Pitfalls:** Overdiagnosis leads to unnecessary antibiotics; underdiagnosis leads to missed complications. This topic regularly appears on ward rounds, in GP, and in exams as a “decision‑making” scenario – when to treat, when to watch, when to escalate.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story:** AOM is essentially a plumbing problem. The Eustachian tube (ET) connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx, normally draining secretions and equalising pressure. When a viral URTI causes mucosal oedema, the ET narrows. In young children, the ET is shorter, more horizontal, and less compliant, making obstruction easy. Negative pressure develops in the middle ear as air is absorbed, pulling in fluid from the mucosa (a transudate) – this is OME. If bacteria colonise that fluid, inflammation spreads → exudative pus → AOM.

**Mechanism → Clinical sign:**

| Pathophysiological step | Clinical sign |
|-------------------------|---------------|
| Viral URTI → ET oedema | Coryza, cough (history) |
| Negative middle ear pressure + fluid accumulation | **Decreased TM mobility** (pneumatic otoscopy) — the TM cannot move because fluid is trapped |
| Bacterial superinfection → pus under pressure | **Bulging TM** — the drum bulges outward; also **otalgia** (pain due to pressure on TM nerve endings) |
| Inflammatory cytokines → vasodilation | **Erythematous TM** — redness from increased blood flow |
| Pressure forces perforation → pus drains | **Otorrhea** — often a sudden decrease in pain, but risk now of middle ear contamination from ear canal |
| Unresolved inflammation → spread to mastoid air cells | **Post‑auricular swelling, tenderness, ear protrusion** — mastoiditis |
| Intracranial extension | **Signs of meningitis, brain abscess, or sinus thrombosis** (rare, late) |

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette (fever + ear pulling + bulging TM) makes sense because pressure causes pain (ear pulling), inflammation causes fever and erythema, and fluid causes bulging. The lack of post‑auricular signs means mastoiditis has not yet occurred. If the child had been left untreated for a week, you would see the progression to mastoiditis.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
**Most common cause:** Bacterial AOM is most often caused by *Streptococcus pneumoniae* (30–40%), non‑typeable *Haemophilus influenzae* (20–30%), and *Moraxella catarrhalis* (10–20%). Viral URTIs (RSV, influenza, rhinovirus) often precede bacterial superinfection.

| Bin | Causes | Stem clue that points to it |
|-----|--------|-----------------------------|
| **Infectious – Bacterial** (most common) | *S. pneumoniae*, *H. influenzae*, *M. catarrhalis*, *Streptococcus pyogenes*, *Staphylococcus aureus* (rare) | Acute onset, high fever, bulging TM, purulent otorrhea; bilateral in young children (<2 y) more likely *H. influenzae* |
| **Infectious – Viral** | RSV, influenza, parainfluenza, adenovirus, rhinovirus | Preceding coryza, cough, low‑grade fever; TM may be more red than bulging; can cause OME without AOM |
| **Anatomical / Host factors** | Eustachian tube dysfunction (cleft palate, Down syndrome, craniofacial anomalies), immunodeficiency, passive smoking | Recurrent AOM, chronic OME, poor response to antibiotics |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Daycare attendance, bottle‑feeding (vs breastfeeding), pacifier use, parental smoking, immunodeficiency | Multiple risk factors in history – a “daycare kid” is classic |

**Key note:** Up to 80% of AOM episodes are preceded by a viral URTI, but that doesn’t change management – the decision to treat with antibiotics is based on severity, age, and laterality.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (Acute Otitis Media – AAO‑HNS guidelines):**
A diagnosis requires **three things**:
1. **Acute onset** (of pain, fever, irritability)
2. **Middle‑ear effusion** (confirmed by bulging TM, decreased mobility, or otorrhea)
3. **Signs of middle‑ear inflammation** (otalgia, fever, erythema of TM)

**No scoring system is commonly used in clinical practice; diagnosis is clinical.**

**Tests (almost always unnecessary in simple AOM):**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Otoscopy + pneumatic insufflation** | TM appearance + mobility | Gold standard for confirming effusion | Bulging + immobile = AOM; only erythema with normal mobility = crying/fever, not AOM (common pitfall) |
| **Tympanometry** | Middle‑ear pressure / compliance | If TM views inadequate (canal debris, uncooperative child) | Flat curve (no compliance) = effusion |
| **Typanocentesis (needle aspiration)** | Culture of middle‑ear fluid | Only in treatment‑failure, immunocompromised, or research | Guides targeted antibiotics |
| **Blood work (CBC, CRP, blood culture)** | Markers of infection | If toxic‑appearing or suspected intracranial extension | WBC >15 + CRP >20 suggests severe bacterial infection |
| **Imaging (CT head)** | Mastoiditis, abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis | Only if complications suspected (post‑auricular swelling, neurological signs, persistent high fever) | Air‑fluid level in mastoid = mastoiditis; ring‑enhancing lesion = abscess |
| **Hearing screen (audiometry)** | Conductive hearing loss | After recurrent AOM or persistent OME >3 months | ≥20 dB loss = language development risk; consider grommets |

**Single finding that confirms AOM:** A **bulging tympanic membrane with decreased mobility** – that is the hallmark. Absent one of these, you cannot be certain it is AOM vs OME or crying‑induced injection.

**Single finding that rules out AOM:** **Normal otoscopy** – no erythema, no bulging, normal mobility. If the child is crying and the TM is red but mobile, it’s not AOM.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Analgesia:** Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg po (max 600 mg) or Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 15 mg/kg po – essential even if planning watchful waiting. Pain is the main symptom.
- **Decision to treat with antibiotics:**
  - **Antibiotics indicated** in any of these:
    - Age <6 months (any AOM)
    - Age 6 months–2 years with **bilateral** AOM or severe symptoms (fever ≥39 °C, severe otalgia)
    - Any age with **otorrhea** (spontaneous perforation)
    - Severe systemic illness (toxic‑appearing)
    - Immunocompromised or at high risk for complications
  - **Watchful waiting (48–72 h)** acceptable for:
    - Unilateral AOM in children ≥6 months with non‑severe symptoms
    - Older children (>2 y) with mild symptoms – parent education, safety‑netting
  - **First‑line antibiotic (when indicated):**
    - **Amoxicillin** 80–90 mg/kg/day po divided q8–12h for **10 days** (5–7 days if ≥6 years with mild‑moderate AOM)
    - **If Penicillin allergy (non‑type I):** Cefdinir 14 mg/kg/day po once daily × 5–10 days; **type I allergy:** Azithromycin 5-day course (10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2–5) – but note increasing resistance
    - **If amoxicillin taken within 30 days or treatment failure after 48–72 h:** consider amoxicillin‑clavulanate (80–90 mg/kg/day of amoxicillin component) to cover β‑lactamase‑producing *H. influenzae* and *M. catarrhalis*
- **What NOT to do:**
  - ❌ Don’t prescribe decongestants, antihistamines, or topical ear drops (unless there is a perforation with otorrhea – then a topical antibiotic drop like ofloxacin may help, but systemic antibiotics are still needed)
  - ❌ Don’t give antibiotics to every child with a red TM – overdiagnosis feeds resistance
  - ❌ Don’t delay antibiotics in toxic‑appearing child waiting for cultures

**Ward (24–72 h) – if admitted (severe, failed oral therapy, complication):**

- **IV antibiotics:** Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV/IM q24h until afebrile and improved, then switch to oral amoxicillin‑clavulanate to complete 10 days
- **ENT consultation** if: mastoiditis, persistent otorrhea >2 weeks, suspicion of cholesteatoma, or need for tympanocentesis
- **Myringotomy** (incision of TM to drain pus) can be done in theatre for decompression if perforation hasn’t occurred and pain is severe
- **Monitoring:** resolution of pain, fever, ear pulling; check for post‑auricular swelling daily

**Long‑term:**

- **Recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in 12 months):** Consider **tympanostomy tubes** (grommets) – they ventilate the middle ear and reduce recurrence during the time they are in place. Also consider prophylactic amoxicillin (20 mg/kg once daily) only if no tubes and patient is high risk.
- **Persistent OME >3 months + hearing loss (≥20 dB) → speech delay:** Adenoidectomy + tympanostomy tubes (especially if age >4 years). Adenoidectomy is believed to reduce ET obstruction and biofilm.
- **High‑risk children (cleft palate, Down syndrome, immunocompromise):** Low‑threshold for tubes, audiology follow‑up, and consider prevention: influenza vaccine, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine, breastfeeding, smoke‑free environment.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acute Otitis Media (AOM) | Otitis Externa (OE) | Mastoiditis | TMJ Dysfunction / Dental Pain |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Deep, pulsatile; worse lying down | Intense pain on moving pinna/tragus | Deep + post‑auricular tenderness | Jaw‑related; worse on chewing or opening mouth |
| **Exam** | Bulging, erythematous TM, reduced mobility | Canal oedema, debris, TM normal (if visible) | Post‑auricular swelling, ear protrusion, TM may be bulging / perforated | Normal ear exam; TM normal, mobility intact |
| **Fever** | Common (≥38 °C) | Uncommon (unless severe/fungal) | Common, high fever possible | Usually absent |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive (mild‑moderate) due to effusion | Conductive due to canal obstruction | Conductive + sensorineural if labyrinth involved | None |
| **Key differentiator** | Pneumatic otoscopy – decreased TM mobility | Tragal pain – normal TM mobility | Post‑auricular erythema and fluctuance | Normal otoscopy, click/popping of TMJ, dental tenderness |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Acute Mastoiditis**
  - **Presentation:** Post‑auricular swelling, erythema, tenderness, ear protrusion (pushed forward and down), persistent high fever, mastoid tenderness on palpation
  - **Key signs:** Loss of post‑auricular sulcus, sagging of posterior–superior ear canal wall
  - **What to do:** Urgent ENT referral, IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + clindamycin if MRSA risk), CT temporal bone, consider mastoidectomy if abscess or coalescent.
- **Intracranial complications (rare but catastrophic):**
  - **Meningitis:** Fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski signs. Lumbar puncture + IV antibiotics.
  - **Brain abscess (temporal lobe, cerebellum):** Headache, focal seizures, altered GCS, hemiparesis, ataxia. CT head + neurosurgery.
  - **Lateral sinus thrombosis:** Severe headache, papilloedema, “picket‑fence” fever (swinging highs and lows), positive Griesinger’s sign (oedema over mastoid). MR venography + anticoagulation (discuss with haematology).
- **Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM)**
  - **Presentation:** Persistent otorrhea >2 weeks despite antibiotics
  - **Key signs:** Central perforation of TM (not marginal – marginal raises concern for cholesteatoma)
  - **What to do:** Aural toilet (suction), topical antibiotic drops (ciprofloxacin), keep water out; if recurrent → ENT and imaging for cholesteatoma; if bilateral → hearing aid assessment.
- **Cholesteatoma**
  - **Late pattern:** Painless, chronic ear discharge with foul smell, hearing loss, and possibly vertigo
  - **Exam:** Squamous debris in attic region, retraction pocket or marginal perforation
  - **Why it matters:** Destroys ossicles, erodes into labyrinth, facial nerve, and lateral sinus. Surgical excision is required.

**What happens if untreated:** Simple AOM usually resolves spontaneously (80% of bacterial AOM within 7–10 days) but with a risk of complications that increase with time and host factors. The exam‑classic warning signs: **“three weeks of ear pain → mastoiditis”** or **“ear pain subsided, then headache + fever returned”** → think brain abscess (the pus decompresses into mastoid then seeds intracranially).

---
sections.9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Clinical Pearl:
**Mnemonic – The AOM diagnosis: “PBB”**  
- **P** = Pain / fever / irritability  
- **B** = Bulging TM  
- **B** = Bad mobility (reduced on pneumatic otoscopy)

**Mnemonic – Antibiotic decision: “ABX”**  
- **A** = Age <6 months → Rx  
- **B** = Bilateral in <2 years → Rx  
- **X** = eXtra severe (fever ≥39°C) or otorrhea → Rx

**Clinical Pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**

1. **Red TM ≠ AOM.** A crying child can have a diffusely red TM because of increased blood flow. Use pneumatic otoscopy – if the TM moves, it’s not AOM. This is the #1 ward mistake.
2. **Bulging TM is the gold standard sign.** Even an inexperienced clinician can identify an outward‑curving, opacified drum. Any “bulging” means effusion.
3. **The child with AOM who stops crying and then starts crying again → perforation may have occurred (relief then return of otorrhea pain).** Look for discharge.
4. **Persistent fever 48 h after starting amoxicillin** → consider resistant *H. influenzae* or *M. catarrhalis* → switch to amoxicillin‑clavulanate or ceftriaxone.
5. **Adult with ear pain + no fever + no bulging → think referred pain: TMJ, dental, Eagle syndrome (elongated styloid).** Don’t label as otitis media without TM findings.
6. **Bilateral AOM in a child <2 years is almost always *H. influenzae* (no pneumococcal vaccine).** In the UK with PCV‑13 this has shifted towards *H. influenzae* and *M. catarrhalis*.
7. **Otoscopic view can be obstructed by wax – don’t force.** Use a curette or wax remover. If you can’t see, treat the risk: if child is well, wait; if toxic, start empiric antibiotics.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Prescribing antibiotics to every child with a “red ear” – watchful waiting is safe and reduces resistance.
- Forgetting to check for mastoid tenderness in a child with seemingly simple AOM and high fever.
- Using topical ear drops alone for AOM – they don’t penetrate an intact TM.
- Repeating courses of amoxicillin for recurrent AOM without considering grommets or adenoidectomy.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to escalate to ENT / senior / ICU:**

| Clinical picture | Action | Threshold |
|-----------------|--------|-----------|
| **Toxic appearance** (high fever, tachycardia, hypotension, ill‑looking) | Immediate IV access, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, senior decision‑maker, ICU or HDU | NEWS2 ≥5, lactate ≥2, or clinical concern |
| **Post‑auricular swelling + ear protrusion** (mastoiditis) | Urgent ENT referral – likely CT head + IV antibiotics + possible mastoidectomy | Do not wait for imaging if clinical signs clear |
| **Focal neurological signs** (facial droop, ataxia, hemiparesis, seizures) | Emergency CT head + neurosurgery consult; IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole) | GCS <15 (especially if drop of 2 points) |
| **Meningism** (neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski) | Immediate septic screen, LP (if safe), IV antibiotics, ICU input | HDU/ICU for monitoring |
| **Persistent high fever 48 h on oral antibiotics** | Re‑check otoscopy; consider tympanocentesis or IV switch; senior review | Temperature >39°C unresponsive |
| **GCS <13** (any cause) | Crash call, CT head, neurosurgery | GCS <13 is urgent; GCS <8 consider airway |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if sepsis suspected) | Sepsis 6, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, ICU referral | NEWS‑based escalation already covers this |

**Common escalations in practice:**
- “I have a child with AOM who looks unwell and has a post‑auricular swelling” → urgent ENT (do not send to GP for clinic next week)
- “Adult with earache, now dizziness and facial weakness” → suspect cholesteatoma or mastoiditis involving facial nerve – immediate ENT

**Bottom line:** If the patient looks sick, the ear is just one piece. Manage the sepsis first, then the ear. Otherwise, AOM is a straightforward condition that resolves with the right diagnosis, pain control, and – when appropriate – a targeted course of amoxicillin.

rawMarkdown:
## Otitis Media

**One-liner:** Acute middle ear infection, most common in young children but all ages at risk; if missed can cause hearing loss, mastoiditis, or intracranial spread.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 2‑year‑old boy presents to the ED with a 2‑day history of fever (38.9 °C), irritability, and pulling at his right ear. His mother says he has had a runny nose for the past week. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is bulging, opaque, and erythematous, with decreased mobility on pneumatic insufflation. He has mild purulent discharge in the ear canal (from a spontaneous perforation). No post‑auricular swelling. He is otherwise well‑appearing.

**Dissect the vignette (pattern clues):**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Age (2 years) | Peak age for acute otitis media (AOM) — 6 months–3 years; Eustachian tube is short, horizontal, and prone to dysfunction |
| Recent upper respiratory infection (coryza) | Viral upper respiratory tract infection (URTI) causes Eustachian tube oedema → impaired middle ear drainage → negative pressure → effusion |
| Fever + ear pulling | Classic combination: fever indicates systemic inflammation; ear pulling is a non‑specific localising sign (babies can’t localise pain) |
| Bulging + opaque TM | Bulging = pus under pressure; opacity = purulent fluid (rather than serous effusion) |
| Erythematous TM | Hyperaemia from inflammation |
| Decreased TM mobility | Confirms presence of fluid/pus in middle ear space |
| Spontaneous otorrhea | Perforation of TM → pus draining into ear canal; often decompresses pain (child may suddenly improve) |
| No post‑auricular swelling | Unlikely mastoiditis (makes sense as this is uncomplicated AOM) |
| Well‑appearing | No signs of systemic toxicity → can be managed without immediate IV antibiotics |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **The afebrile adult:** A 30‑year‑old with deep earache, normal temperature, and mild TM injection. Could be early AOM, but also think of TMJ dysfunction, dental pain, or otitis externa. The key clue: TM mobility is reduced (pus) vs normal in non‑infectious causes.
- **The immunocompromised patient:** Neutropenic or HIV‑positive patient with no fever, minimal ear pain, but a bulging TM with necrotic appearance. This is a medical emergency — pseudomonal or fungal infection can rapidly cause malignant otitis externa or extend intracranially.
- **The “grumpy baby”:** A 6‑month‑old with only poor feeding and crying, no fever, no ear pulling. Parents may not notice ear symptoms. Always complete an otoscopic exam on any febrile infant with unexplained irritability.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Otitis media is inflammation of the middle ear mucosa. When it is acute and infected (acute otitis media, AOM), a pocket of pus builds up behind the eardrum, causing pain, fever, and hearing loss. It is distinct from otitis media with effusion (OME), where fluid sits behind the TM without acute infection.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** AOM is the most common bacterial infection in children — about 80% of children will have at least one episode by age 3. It accounts for massive antibiotic use and clinic visits.
- **Stakes:** Hearing loss from AOM or persistent OME can delay language development. Complications — mastoiditis, meningitis, brain abscess — are rare but devastating.
- **Pitfalls:** Overdiagnosis leads to unnecessary antibiotics; underdiagnosis leads to missed complications. This topic regularly appears on ward rounds, in GP, and in exams as a “decision‑making” scenario – when to treat, when to watch, when to escalate.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story:** AOM is essentially a plumbing problem. The Eustachian tube (ET) connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx, normally draining secretions and equalising pressure. When a viral URTI causes mucosal oedema, the ET narrows. In young children, the ET is shorter, more horizontal, and less compliant, making obstruction easy. Negative pressure develops in the middle ear as air is absorbed, pulling in fluid from the mucosa (a transudate) – this is OME. If bacteria colonise that fluid, inflammation spreads → exudative pus → AOM.

**Mechanism → Clinical sign:**

| Pathophysiological step | Clinical sign |
|-------------------------|---------------|
| Viral URTI → ET oedema | Coryza, cough (history) |
| Negative middle ear pressure + fluid accumulation | **Decreased TM mobility** (pneumatic otoscopy) — the TM cannot move because fluid is trapped |
| Bacterial superinfection → pus under pressure | **Bulging TM** — the drum bulges outward; also **otalgia** (pain due to pressure on TM nerve endings) |
| Inflammatory cytokines → vasodilation | **Erythematous TM** — redness from increased blood flow |
| Pressure forces perforation → pus drains | **Otorrhea** — often a sudden decrease in pain, but risk now of middle ear contamination from ear canal |
| Unresolved inflammation → spread to mastoid air cells | **Post‑auricular swelling, tenderness, ear protrusion** — mastoiditis |
| Intracranial extension | **Signs of meningitis, brain abscess, or sinus thrombosis** (rare, late) |

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The classic vignette (fever + ear pulling + bulging TM) makes sense because pressure causes pain (ear pulling), inflammation causes fever and erythema, and fluid causes bulging. The lack of post‑auricular signs means mastoiditis has not yet occurred. If the child had been left untreated for a week, you would see the progression to mastoiditis.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

**Most common cause:** Bacterial AOM is most often caused by *Streptococcus pneumoniae* (30–40%), non‑typeable *Haemophilus influenzae* (20–30%), and *Moraxella catarrhalis* (10–20%). Viral URTIs (RSV, influenza, rhinovirus) often precede bacterial superinfection.

| Bin | Causes | Stem clue that points to it |
|-----|--------|-----------------------------|
| **Infectious – Bacterial** (most common) | *S. pneumoniae*, *H. influenzae*, *M. catarrhalis*, *Streptococcus pyogenes*, *Staphylococcus aureus* (rare) | Acute onset, high fever, bulging TM, purulent otorrhea; bilateral in young children (<2 y) more likely *H. influenzae* |
| **Infectious – Viral** | RSV, influenza, parainfluenza, adenovirus, rhinovirus | Preceding coryza, cough, low‑grade fever; TM may be more red than bulging; can cause OME without AOM |
| **Anatomical / Host factors** | Eustachian tube dysfunction (cleft palate, Down syndrome, craniofacial anomalies), immunodeficiency, passive smoking | Recurrent AOM, chronic OME, poor response to antibiotics |
| **Environmental / Lifestyle** | Daycare attendance, bottle‑feeding (vs breastfeeding), pacifier use, parental smoking, immunodeficiency | Multiple risk factors in history – a “daycare kid” is classic |

**Key note:** Up to 80% of AOM episodes are preceded by a viral URTI, but that doesn’t change management – the decision to treat with antibiotics is based on severity, age, and laterality.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (Acute Otitis Media – AAO‑HNS guidelines):**
A diagnosis requires **three things**:
1. **Acute onset** (of pain, fever, irritability)
2. **Middle‑ear effusion** (confirmed by bulging TM, decreased mobility, or otorrhea)
3. **Signs of middle‑ear inflammation** (otalgia, fever, erythema of TM)

**No scoring system is commonly used in clinical practice; diagnosis is clinical.**

**Tests (almost always unnecessary in simple AOM):**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Otoscopy + pneumatic insufflation** | TM appearance + mobility | Gold standard for confirming effusion | Bulging + immobile = AOM; only erythema with normal mobility = crying/fever, not AOM (common pitfall) |
| **Tympanometry** | Middle‑ear pressure / compliance | If TM views inadequate (canal debris, uncooperative child) | Flat curve (no compliance) = effusion |
| **Typanocentesis (needle aspiration)** | Culture of middle‑ear fluid | Only in treatment‑failure, immunocompromised, or research | Guides targeted antibiotics |
| **Blood work (CBC, CRP, blood culture)** | Markers of infection | If toxic‑appearing or suspected intracranial extension | WBC >15 + CRP >20 suggests severe bacterial infection |
| **Imaging (CT head)** | Mastoiditis, abscess, sigmoid sinus thrombosis | Only if complications suspected (post‑auricular swelling, neurological signs, persistent high fever) | Air‑fluid level in mastoid = mastoiditis; ring‑enhancing lesion = abscess |
| **Hearing screen (audiometry)** | Conductive hearing loss | After recurrent AOM or persistent OME >3 months | ≥20 dB loss = language development risk; consider grommets |

**Single finding that confirms AOM:** A **bulging tympanic membrane with decreased mobility** – that is the hallmark. Absent one of these, you cannot be certain it is AOM vs OME or crying‑induced injection.

**Single finding that rules out AOM:** **Normal otoscopy** – no erythema, no bulging, normal mobility. If the child is crying and the TM is red but mobile, it’s not AOM.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Analgesia:** Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg po (max 600 mg) or Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 15 mg/kg po – essential even if planning watchful waiting. Pain is the main symptom.
- **Decision to treat with antibiotics:**
  - **Antibiotics indicated** in any of these:
    - Age <6 months (any AOM)
    - Age 6 months–2 years with **bilateral** AOM or severe symptoms (fever ≥39 °C, severe otalgia)
    - Any age with **otorrhea** (spontaneous perforation)
    - Severe systemic illness (toxic‑appearing)
    - Immunocompromised or at high risk for complications
  - **Watchful waiting (48–72 h)** acceptable for:
    - Unilateral AOM in children ≥6 months with non‑severe symptoms
    - Older children (>2 y) with mild symptoms – parent education, safety‑netting
  - **First‑line antibiotic (when indicated):**
    - **Amoxicillin** 80–90 mg/kg/day po divided q8–12h for **10 days** (5–7 days if ≥6 years with mild‑moderate AOM)
    - **If Penicillin allergy (non‑type I):** Cefdinir 14 mg/kg/day po once daily × 5–10 days; **type I allergy:** Azithromycin 5-day course (10 mg/kg day 1, then 5 mg/kg days 2–5) – but note increasing resistance
    - **If amoxicillin taken within 30 days or treatment failure after 48–72 h:** consider amoxicillin‑clavulanate (80–90 mg/kg/day of amoxicillin component) to cover β‑lactamase‑producing *H. influenzae* and *M. catarrhalis*
- **What NOT to do:**
  - ❌ Don’t prescribe decongestants, antihistamines, or topical ear drops (unless there is a perforation with otorrhea – then a topical antibiotic drop like ofloxacin may help, but systemic antibiotics are still needed)
  - ❌ Don’t give antibiotics to every child with a red TM – overdiagnosis feeds resistance
  - ❌ Don’t delay antibiotics in toxic‑appearing child waiting for cultures

**Ward (24–72 h) – if admitted (severe, failed oral therapy, complication):**

- **IV antibiotics:** Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV/IM q24h until afebrile and improved, then switch to oral amoxicillin‑clavulanate to complete 10 days
- **ENT consultation** if: mastoiditis, persistent otorrhea >2 weeks, suspicion of cholesteatoma, or need for tympanocentesis
- **Myringotomy** (incision of TM to drain pus) can be done in theatre for decompression if perforation hasn’t occurred and pain is severe
- **Monitoring:** resolution of pain, fever, ear pulling; check for post‑auricular swelling daily

**Long‑term:**

- **Recurrent AOM (≥3 episodes in 6 months or ≥4 in 12 months):** Consider **tympanostomy tubes** (grommets) – they ventilate the middle ear and reduce recurrence during the time they are in place. Also consider prophylactic amoxicillin (20 mg/kg once daily) only if no tubes and patient is high risk.
- **Persistent OME >3 months + hearing loss (≥20 dB) → speech delay:** Adenoidectomy + tympanostomy tubes (especially if age >4 years). Adenoidectomy is believed to reduce ET obstruction and biofilm.
- **High‑risk children (cleft palate, Down syndrome, immunocompromise):** Low‑threshold for tubes, audiology follow‑up, and consider prevention: influenza vaccine, pneumococcal conjugate vaccine, breastfeeding, smoke‑free environment.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acute Otitis Media (AOM) | Otitis Externa (OE) | Mastoiditis | TMJ Dysfunction / Dental Pain |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Deep, pulsatile; worse lying down | Intense pain on moving pinna/tragus | Deep + post‑auricular tenderness | Jaw‑related; worse on chewing or opening mouth |
| **Exam** | Bulging, erythematous TM, reduced mobility | Canal oedema, debris, TM normal (if visible) | Post‑auricular swelling, ear protrusion, TM may be bulging / perforated | Normal ear exam; TM normal, mobility intact |
| **Fever** | Common (≥38 °C) | Uncommon (unless severe/fungal) | Common, high fever possible | Usually absent |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive (mild‑moderate) due to effusion | Conductive due to canal obstruction | Conductive + sensorineural if labyrinth involved | None |
| **Key differentiator** | Pneumatic otoscopy – decreased TM mobility | Tragal pain – normal TM mobility | Post‑auricular erythema and fluctuance | Normal otoscopy, click/popping of TMJ, dental tenderness |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Acute Mastoiditis**
  - **Presentation:** Post‑auricular swelling, erythema, tenderness, ear protrusion (pushed forward and down), persistent high fever, mastoid tenderness on palpation
  - **Key signs:** Loss of post‑auricular sulcus, sagging of posterior–superior ear canal wall
  - **What to do:** Urgent ENT referral, IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + clindamycin if MRSA risk), CT temporal bone, consider mastoidectomy if abscess or coalescent.
- **Intracranial complications (rare but catastrophic):**
  - **Meningitis:** Fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski signs. Lumbar puncture + IV antibiotics.
  - **Brain abscess (temporal lobe, cerebellum):** Headache, focal seizures, altered GCS, hemiparesis, ataxia. CT head + neurosurgery.
  - **Lateral sinus thrombosis:** Severe headache, papilloedema, “picket‑fence” fever (swinging highs and lows), positive Griesinger’s sign (oedema over mastoid). MR venography + anticoagulation (discuss with haematology).
- **Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM)**
  - **Presentation:** Persistent otorrhea >2 weeks despite antibiotics
  - **Key signs:** Central perforation of TM (not marginal – marginal raises concern for cholesteatoma)
  - **What to do:** Aural toilet (suction), topical antibiotic drops (ciprofloxacin), keep water out; if recurrent → ENT and imaging for cholesteatoma; if bilateral → hearing aid assessment.
- **Cholesteatoma**
  - **Late pattern:** Painless, chronic ear discharge with foul smell, hearing loss, and possibly vertigo
  - **Exam:** Squamous debris in attic region, retraction pocket or marginal perforation
  - **Why it matters:** Destroys ossicles, erodes into labyrinth, facial nerve, and lateral sinus. Surgical excision is required.

**What happens if untreated:** Simple AOM usually resolves spontaneously (80% of bacterial AOM within 7–10 days) but with a risk of complications that increase with time and host factors. The exam‑classic warning signs: **“three weeks of ear pain → mastoiditis”** or **“ear pain subsided, then headache + fever returned”** → think brain abscess (the pus decompresses into mastoid then seeds intracranially).

---

### 9. High‑Yield Mnemonics & Clinical Pearls

**Mnemonic – The AOM diagnosis: “PBB”**  
- **P** = Pain / fever / irritability  
- **B** = Bulging TM  
- **B** = Bad mobility (reduced on pneumatic otoscopy)

**Mnemonic – Antibiotic decision: “ABX”**  
- **A** = Age <6 months → Rx  
- **B** = Bilateral in <2 years → Rx  
- **X** = eXtra severe (fever ≥39°C) or otorrhea → Rx

**Clinical Pearls (pattern‑recognition cues):**

1. **Red TM ≠ AOM.** A crying child can have a diffusely red TM because of increased blood flow. Use pneumatic otoscopy – if the TM moves, it’s not AOM. This is the #1 ward mistake.
2. **Bulging TM is the gold standard sign.** Even an inexperienced clinician can identify an outward‑curving, opacified drum. Any “bulging” means effusion.
3. **The child with AOM who stops crying and then starts crying again → perforation may have occurred (relief then return of otorrhea pain).** Look for discharge.
4. **Persistent fever 48 h after starting amoxicillin** → consider resistant *H. influenzae* or *M. catarrhalis* → switch to amoxicillin‑clavulanate or ceftriaxone.
5. **Adult with ear pain + no fever + no bulging → think referred pain: TMJ, dental, Eagle syndrome (elongated styloid).** Don’t label as otitis media without TM findings.
6. **Bilateral AOM in a child <2 years is almost always *H. influenzae* (no pneumococcal vaccine).** In the UK with PCV‑13 this has shifted towards *H. influenzae* and *M. catarrhalis*.
7. **Otoscopic view can be obstructed by wax – don’t force.** Use a curette or wax remover. If you can’t see, treat the risk: if child is well, wait; if toxic, start empiric antibiotics.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Prescribing antibiotics to every child with a “red ear” – watchful waiting is safe and reduces resistance.
- Forgetting to check for mastoid tenderness in a child with seemingly simple AOM and high fever.
- Using topical ear drops alone for AOM – they don’t penetrate an intact TM.
- Repeating courses of amoxicillin for recurrent AOM without considering grommets or adenoidectomy.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to escalate to ENT / senior / ICU:**

| Clinical picture | Action | Threshold |
|-----------------|--------|-----------|
| **Toxic appearance** (high fever, tachycardia, hypotension, ill‑looking) | Immediate IV access, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, senior decision‑maker, ICU or HDU | NEWS2 ≥5, lactate ≥2, or clinical concern |
| **Post‑auricular swelling + ear protrusion** (mastoiditis) | Urgent ENT referral – likely CT head + IV antibiotics + possible mastoidectomy | Do not wait for imaging if clinical signs clear |
| **Focal neurological signs** (facial droop, ataxia, hemiparesis, seizures) | Emergency CT head + neurosurgery consult; IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole) | GCS <15 (especially if drop of 2 points) |
| **Meningism** (neck stiffness, photophobia, Kernig/Brudzinski) | Immediate septic screen, LP (if safe), IV antibiotics, ICU input | HDU/ICU for monitoring |
| **Persistent high fever 48 h on oral antibiotics** | Re‑check otoscopy; consider tympanocentesis or IV switch; senior review | Temperature >39°C unresponsive |
| **GCS <13** (any cause) | Crash call, CT head, neurosurgery | GCS <13 is urgent; GCS <8 consider airway |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if sepsis suspected) | Sepsis 6, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, ICU referral | NEWS‑based escalation already covers this |

**Common escalations in practice:**
- “I have a child with AOM who looks unwell and has a post‑auricular swelling” → urgent ENT (do not send to GP for clinic next week)
- “Adult with earache, now dizziness and facial weakness” → suspect cholesteatoma or mastoiditis involving facial nerve – immediate ENT

**Bottom line:** If the patient looks sick, the ear is just one piece. Manage the sepsis first, then the ear. Otherwise, AOM is a straightforward condition that resolves with the right diagnosis, pain control, and – when appropriate – a targeted course of amoxicillin.
